content:
14 min read
20 hours ago

--

Hot Module Replacement (HMR) is a development feature that allows an application to replace only the modified module or component at runtime without performing a full page reload.
Instead of restarting the entire application, the development server sends the updated module to the browser, which patches the running application.
The statement:
“Angular’s general JavaScript-based HMR is currently not supported, while HMR is available for templates and styles.”
Angular 19+ (using the Vite/esbuild build system) supports Hot Module Replacement for component templates and styles. Changes to TypeScript files (components, services, directives, pipes, configuration, etc.) still trigger a full application reload because JavaScript/TypeScript HMR is not currently supported.
Here’s the version timeline:

• v16 and earlier : Webpack-based HMR (experimental/manual). Full JavaScript HMR was never officially supported.

• v17 : Introduced the new Vite/esbuild build system (opt-in). Only stylesheet HMR was available initially. https://v17.angular.io/guide/esbuild

• v18 : New build system became more mature, but JavaScript/TypeScript HMR was still unavailable. https://angular.dev/tools/cli/build-system-migration

• v19 : Template HMR and CSS HMR introduced. Changes to .ts files still trigger a full reload. https://angular.dev/roadmap

• v20 : Template HMR became stable. Styles and templates hot-reload, but TypeScript (component/service/directive/pipe) changes still cause a full application reload. https://angular.dev/roadmap
Why isn’t TypeScript HMR supported?
Angular’s dependency injection, compiled component metadata, and runtime references make replacing TypeScript classes safely much more complex than in frameworks like React. Because of this, the Angular team currently supports HMR only for templates and styles, where updates can be applied safely without recreating application state.
What happens today (Angular 20)
| File Changed | HMR? | Result |
| ------------------------ | ----- | ---------------------------- |
| `app.component.html` | ✅ Yes | Updates instantly |
| `app.component.css/scss` | ✅ Yes | Styles update without reload |
| Inline template | ✅ Yes | Updates instantly |
| Inline styles | ✅ Yes | Updates instantly |
| `app.component.ts` | ❌ No | Full application reload |
| Service `.ts` | ❌ No | Full reload |
| Directive `.ts` | ❌ No | Full reload |
| Pipe `.ts` | ❌ No | Full reload |
| `app.config.ts` | ❌ No | Full reload |
| Environment/config files | ❌ No | Full reload |Hot Module Replacement (HMR) is a development capability that updates only the modified component or module in a running application instead of reloading the entire page. Before HMR, developers relied on live reload, where every code change caused a full application restart, losing runtime state such as form inputs or navigation context. HMR addresses this by recompiling only the changed module, notifying the browser through the development server, and replacing the affected module at runtime. This significantly shortens the feedback loop and improves developer productivity, especially in large Angular applications with hundreds of components. It’s important to note that HMR is a development-only feature; production builds do not include it because its purpose is to improve the developer experience rather than application runtime performance.
Run
ng serve or npm startnpm start
> demo@0.0.0 start
> ng serveComponent HMR has been enabled, see https://angular.dev/hmr for more info.
The message:
Component HMR has been enabled, see https://angular.dev/hmr for more info.is not coming from your application code. It is printed by the Angular CLI (ng serve) itself.
Where does it come from?
When you run:
npm startyour package.json probably contains:
{
"scripts": {
"start": "ng serve"
}
}ng serve starts the Angular development server, and in recent Angular versions (Angular 17+), Component HMR (Hot Module Replacement) is enabled by default.
During startup, the Angular CLI prints:
Component HMR has been enabled, see https://angular.dev/hmr for more info.This log originates from the Angular Dev Server (@angular/build / previously @angular-devkit/build-angular), not from your project's source files.

Why Angular 17/18/19+ still feels much faster
Even though TypeScript changes still trigger a full reload, the development experience is significantly improved because:

• esbuild compiles TypeScript much faster than Webpack.

• Vite serves native ES modules and only rebuilds affected modules instead of rebundling the whole application.

• Template and style edits are applied with HMR, avoiding a reload for the most frequent UI tweaks.

• The development server starts much more quickly, and incremental rebuilds are substantially faster.
So, the performance gains come from faster compilation and smarter module updates, not from full HMR support for all TypeScript changes.
Angular 16 & Earlier we had javascript HMR then why still reloading full page ?
The answer is that Angular had HMR support before Angular 17, but it was not automatic or complete.
There are actually three different eras of HMR in Angular:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why did Angular 16 reload the full page?
By default, ng serve did not enable HMR.
Normal development flow:
Save component.ts
│
▼
Webpack rebuilds
│
▼
Browser refreshes page
│
▼
Angular bootstraps againThis is what most Angular developers experienced.
But Angular 16 had HMR…
Yes.
If you started the application like:
ng serve --hmrWebpack enabled Hot Module Replacement.
However, Angular itself didn’t automatically know how to preserve component state or swap modules safely. Developers typically had to add HMR support in their bootstrap code, for example:
if (module['hot']) {
module['hot'].accept();
module['hot'].dispose(() => {
// cleanup
});
}That is why it was considered experimental/manual.
Why wasn’t it seamless?
Webpack HMR can replace JavaScript modules, but Angular applications have runtime state:

• Dependency Injection container

• Component tree

• Change detection

• Router state

• RxJS subscriptions

• Forms

• Signals (later versions)
Simply replacing a .ts module doesn't automatically reconnect all of these.
For example:
CounterComponent
count = 25You save:
count = 30;Webpack can replace the JavaScript module.
But then Angular has questions like:

• Should it recreate the component?

• Should it keep count = 25?

• Should it destroy subscriptions?

• Should it recreate child components?
Webpack doesn’t know the answers because it’s framework-agnostic.
Why is Angular 17+ different?
Angular now integrates with Vite and provides framework-aware HMR for templates and styles.
Template change
Save component.html
│
▼
Angular recompiles template
│
▼
Vite pushes update
│
▼
Angular patches component viewNo application restart.
Style change
Save component.scss
│
▼
Inject new CSS
│
▼
DoneNo restart.
TypeScript change
Save component.ts
│
▼
Angular cannot safely replace arbitrary logic
│
▼
Full reloadThis is still the default because changes to TypeScript can affect dependency injection, lifecycle hooks, decorators, metadata, or application behavior in ways that aren’t always safe to patch live.
So was JavaScript HMR in Angular 16 “real”?
Yes — but it was Webpack’s generic HMR, not Angular-specific HMR.
Webpack
│
├── Replace JavaScript modules ✅
│
└── Doesn't understand Angular component lifecycle ❌Angular 17+ instead provides HMR that’s aware of Angular constructs:
Vite
│
▼
Angular HMR
│
├── Templates ✅
├── Styles ✅
└── Arbitrary TypeScript logic ❌ (reload)Summary

• Angular 16 and earlier: HMR existed through Webpack and could be enabled with ng serve --hmr, but it was optional and often required additional code. Many TypeScript changes still resulted in a full reload because Angular couldn't safely hot-swap all runtime state.

• Angular 17–20: HMR is built into the Vite-based development workflow. Template and style changes are updated without reloading the app, while most TypeScript changes still trigger a full page reload to ensure correctness.
How does Angular HMR work?
Angular 16 & Earlier (Webpack HMR)
Technology: Webpack Dev Server + Webpack HMR Runtime
HMR Flow
Developer saves file
│
▼
Webpack detects file change
│
▼
Recompile affected modules
(TypeScript → JavaScript)
│
▼
Generate updated Webpack module
│
▼
Webpack HMR Runtime
checks whether module is hot-replaceable
│
├───────────────┐
│ │
▼ ▼
Module accepts HMR? Module doesn't accept HMR
│ │
▼ ▼
Send update Trigger Full Page Reload
via WebSocket
│
▼
Browser downloads updated module
│
▼
Execute module.accept()
(if implemented)
│
▼
Angular attempts to recreate
affected component/module
│
▼
UI updatesCharacteristics

• Uses Webpack HMR Runtime.

• Requires ng serve --hmr.

• Often needs module.hot.accept() (or Angular HMR bootstrap support).

• Not automatic for every component.

• If a module doesn’t accept HMR, Webpack falls back to a full page reload.
Angular 17/18/19+ (Vite + Angular HMR)
Technology: Vite Dev Server + esbuild + Angular Compiler
